caveats: a: Occupation shares are not task shares, and exposure does not imply full job removal.
a: The broad NAICS 532 proxy contains activities with different equipment weights and customer workflows.
rho: Implementation means sustained production use, not merely buying software.
rho: Small seasonal operators may adopt consumer AI tools informally without measurable system integration.

e: Seasonality, permits, weather, and owner dependence can make nominally eligible operators unattractive or non-transferable.

s5: The survey spans industries, sizes, and transfer types beyond the target lens.

q: Higher utilization can increase maintenance, replacement, and handling costs.

d5: Two recent growth observations are not a five-year forecast and may reflect post-pandemic normalization.

o: Liability and safety requirements vary sharply across bicycles, boats, motorcycles, snow gear, and beach equipment.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumers and visitor groups pay time-based rental fees, often by hour or day and with strong seasonal and location effects; operators may also earn delivery, damage-waiver, instruction, or accessory revenue. The model requires local inventory utilization, safe handoff, condition control, and turnaround between users.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive rental desks inside resorts or marinas that are not separable businesses; peer-to-peer marketplaces that do not own or operate the inventory; equipment sales and tours as the primary business; shell, non-control, and subscale hobby operations; and adjacent sporting-goods, vehicle, or accommodation activities classified elsewhere.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operators that own and rent recreational equipment such as bicycles, canoes, motorcycles, skis, sailboats, beach chairs, and umbrellas, including the reservations, fitting or handoff, inspection, maintenance, and retrieval work needed to support repeated rentals.

a.bridge: BLS occupational employment shares for broad NAICS 532 show sizable counter/rental-clerk, sales, administrative, repair, and material-moving work. I map most reservation intake, quoting, scheduling, marketing, routine customer communication, billing, and inventory-allocation tasks to AI exposure, while leaving fitting, safety briefings, cleaning, inspection, repair, transport, and physical handoff unexposed. The broad 532 mix includes vehicle and commercial-equipment lessors, which likely overstates heavy repair and transport versus this industry but may understate its seasonal customer-service burden; the range covers both errors.
caveats: Occupation shares are not task shares, and exposure does not imply full job removal.
The broad NAICS 532 proxy contains activities with different equipment weights and customer workflows.
a.change_evidence: An exact-industry task-and-hours study splitting reservations, sales, administration, fitting, cleaning, maintenance, delivery, and supervision would materially tighten the estimate.
a.rationale: A meaningful front-office layer is language- and workflow-intensive, but the service is anchored by physical, safety-sensitive equipment work that AI cannot directly perform.

d5.bridge: BEA reports real outdoor-recreation GDP growth of 2.7% in 2024 after 5.3% in 2023, while Census reports broad consumer-goods-rental revenue up 5.9% year over year for the first half of 2025 in nominal, not seasonally adjusted terms. I treat these as directional evidence for recreation participation and rental-category demand, strip out an unknown inflation component, and moderate the recent rates over five years. Outdoor-recreation GDP includes travel, owned equipment, and activities unrelated to rental, which can overstate exact-industry quantity growth; substitution from ownership toward rental and access can instead make the exact industry grow faster.
caveats: The Census series is broader than 532284 and is nominal.
Two recent growth observations are not a five-year forecast and may reflect post-pandemic normalization.
d5.change_evidence: Exact 532284 real revenue, rental-days, utilization, and same-location volume by equipment class would materially change the five-year demand ratio.
d5.rationale: Participation and visitor activity support modest real demand growth, but the category remains cyclical, weather-exposed, and vulnerable to discretionary-spending pressure and gear ownership substitutes.

caveats: Establishments are not firms, and multi-location operators may be counted more than once in source datasets.
Seasonality, permits, weather, and owner dependence can make nominally eligible operators unattractive or non-transferable.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of revenue mix, ownership, EBITDA, and stand-alone status for establishments in the code would materially revise eligibility.
e.rationale: The exact NAICS definition is predominantly an external rental activity that fits the operating-company lens, so most economically material firms should be eligible. The estimate removes captive resort or marina desks, owner-dependent hobby operators, and businesses whose economics are primarily tours, retail sales, or adjacent classifications. The supplied establishment-derived count is only a rough anchor because no clean industry-specific margin observation was available.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 532284 ($780,454,798) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 532284 ($2,415,006,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3000 (margins.json[532]: ESTIMATE: rental & leasing is asset-heavy with no clean Damodaran bucket (REITs 43.79% overstates operating rental); 30% chosen as a round mid-point before deriving results); revenue/employee $157,012 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 532284); class <5: 2,011 firms, avg revenue/firm $320,105 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $96,031 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 362 firms, avg revenue/firm $934,655 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $280,396 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 231 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,750,450 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $525,135 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 129 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,635,930 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,090,779 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,865,364 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,059,609 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,002,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,200,600 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 151

caveats: Operator need can persist while labor hours per rental decline.
Liability and safety requirements vary sharply across bicycles, boats, motorcycles, snow gear, and beach equipment.
o.change_evidence: Share data for unattended rental, peer-to-peer platforms, venue-owned fleets, and operator-free equipment access by recreational category would materially change this value.
o.rationale: At year five, most quantity still requires an accountable operator to own or control inventory, verify condition, manage loss and liability, perform maintenance, and support safe fitting or handoff. Self-service lockers, app-mediated access, peer-to-peer supply, direct purchase, and venue-owned fleets can remove some incumbent-operated quantity but do not eliminate accountability for the physical asset.

caveats: No industry study isolates AI-created benefit from ordinary digitization or demand recovery.
Higher utilization can increase maintenance, replacement, and handling costs.
q.change_evidence: Matched operator cohorts showing realized labor savings, utilization lift, price changes, software fees, and competitive pass-through after workflow automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Operators set discrete hourly or daily prices and can retain benefits through higher inventory utilization, reduced administrative time, fewer missed inquiries, and improved scheduling. Retention is limited by local competition, online marketplace commissions, customer price transparency, wage reassignment, safety checks, damage losses, and seasonal capacity constraints.

caveats: Implementation means sustained production use, not merely buying software.
Small seasonal operators may adopt consumer AI tools informally without measurable system integration.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor penetration, feature-usage, and customer-level adoption cohorts for recreational-rental software and AI-assisted workflows would materially change the implementation path.
rho.rationale: Reservation, pricing, waiver, customer-service, marketing, scheduling, and inventory tools can be deployed through existing cloud software, but fragmented owner-managed firms, seasonal cash flow, legacy processes, safety review, and integration with point-of-sale and fleet records slow five-year implementation.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners intended to sell, transfer, or go public within five years. I haircut that intention rate for plans that are delayed or abandoned and for family/internal transfers that do not create a qualifying control opportunity, then allow a wide range for this seasonal, often owner-operated industry. The broad owner survey may overstate this industry's external-sale probability if recreational-rental businesses are more lifestyle-dependent, but it may understate it if aging owners and consolidation create above-average exits.
caveats: Intentions are not completed transactions.
The survey spans industries, sizes, and transfer types beyond the target lens.
s5.change_evidence: Exact-industry broker listings, closed transaction counts, owner-age distribution, and the share of internal versus external transfers would materially change this value.
s5.rationale: The five-year supply of qualifying control transfers is likely well below stated succession intentions but still material among established local operators.

principal_driver: Digital reservation, pricing, customer-service, and inventory decisions can raise conversion and utilization across repeated uses of the same physical asset.
principal_weakness: A large share of work and risk remains local, seasonal, physical, and safety-sensitive, while the available firm-count and demand evidence are indirect.
